You can definitely solve this problem without setting extra environment variables.
The Flask CLI already knows when it is running a command versus serving requests.
You can check the current context to see if a CLI command is executing.
This approach allows you to detect the execution mode dynamically and easily.
You might want to look at the `click` context that Flask uses internally.
There are many ways to pass parameters to your `create_app` function.
One method is to use the `app_context` to inspect the current state.
You can add a custom parameter to your CLI command definition directly.
This parameter can be passed to your app factory when the command runs.
The server mode will simply ignore these CLI-specific parameters.
You do not need to rely on external environment variables for this logic.
The `flask run` command will use the standard configuration settings.
The `flask my_command` invocation can trigger a different initialization path.
You can create a special flag in your app factory to handle this.
This flag can be set only when the CLI command is detected.
The `click` library provides a way to access the command name.
You can use this name to decide which configuration to load.
Your `create_app` function can accept an optional argument for this.
This argument can be passed from the CLI command decorator itself.
You might also want to use a custom `with_appcontext` decorator.
This allows you to inject specific data into the app context.
The `app.cli` object is very flexible for such custom behaviors.
You can define a wrapper function to handle the logic cleanly.
This wrapper will call `create_app` with the correct parameters.
The server will not see these special CLI parameters at all.
